question: The primary purpose of accounting is to:
options: Minimize taxes for businesses and individuals
Provide financial information to decision-makers — allowing managers, investors, lenders, and regulators to understand a business's financial condition and performance
Count physical inventory in a warehouse
Determine employee salaries and compensation packages
explanation: Accounting is an information system. It systematically records, summarizes, and reports financial data so that decision-makers can assess performance, allocate resources, and evaluate risk. Tax minimization is one use, but it is secondary to the fundamental purpose of providing accurate financial information.

question: Financial accounting differs from managerial accounting in that financial accounting:
options: Is used only internally by management to make decisions

Reports to external parties (investors, creditors, regulators) using standardized GAAP rules; managerial accounting provides customized internal data for management decisions without needing to follow specific external standards
Is only required for publicly traded companies
Focuses only on future financial projections
explanation: Financial accounting is external and standardized (GAAP/IFRS) — it produces the income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement that outside stakeholders rely on. Managerial accounting is internal and flexible — it produces cost analyses, budgets, and variance reports customized to management needs. Both use the same underlying data but serve different audiences with different formats.

question: GAAP stands for Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. Why must public companies follow GAAP?
options: GAAP maximizes profitability for all companies that follow it

GAAP standardizes financial reporting so that investors and analysts can compare financial statements across companies and time periods — without common standards, each company could report numbers in incomparable ways
GAAP is required only for companies that pay dividends
GAAP automatically prevents financial fraud
explanation: GAAP exists to enable comparability. If Apple can recognize revenue differently from Microsoft, investors cannot meaningfully compare their financials. Standardized rules (revenue recognition, depreciation, inventory valuation) create a common language. GAAP doesn't prevent fraud — it sets the standards that auditors use to catch deviations.

question: An auditor reviews a company's financial statements. The auditor's primary role is to:
options: Prepare the financial statements on behalf of the company
Provide an independent opinion on whether the financial statements fairly present the company's financial position according to GAAP — providing credibility to the numbers for external stakeholders
Set the company's accounting policies
Guarantee that the company is profitable
explanation: Auditors provide independent verification — they are third parties who confirm that financial statements follow GAAP and are free from material misstatement. They don't prepare the statements (management does) or guarantee accuracy (an 'unqualified opinion' means nothing came to their attention suggesting misstatement, not a guarantee). Auditor independence is essential to the credibility of financial reporting.

question: A bank evaluating a loan application for a restaurant requests three years of financial statements. Which primary use of accounting does this represent?
options: Tax preparation — the bank wants to verify tax payments
Investor equity analysis — the bank is considering buying the restaurant
Credit assessment — lenders use financial statements to evaluate the borrower's ability to repay: revenue trends, profitability, existing debt, and cash flow generation
Employee performance evaluation — the bank is assessing the chef's productivity
explanation: Lenders are one of the most important users of financial accounting. They use financial statements to evaluate creditworthiness: Are revenues stable and growing? Is there sufficient cash flow to service debt payments? What is the debt-to-equity ratio? How profitable is the business? The standardization of GAAP financial statements is what makes them useful for credit decisions across diverse businesses.

question: Under the accrual basis of accounting, revenue is recognized when:
options: Cash is received from the customer
The customer places an order
The good is delivered or the service is performed, regardless of when cash is received
The accounting period ends (quarterly or annually)
explanation: Accrual accounting records revenue when it is earned (delivery/performance), not when cash arrives. A company that delivers $500,000 of services in December but receives payment in January records the revenue in December. This provides a more accurate picture of economic activity, but creates the possibility that reported profit exceeds actual cash on hand.

question: A construction company signs a $2M contract in March, starts work in April, completes 50% by June 30, and finishes in September. Under percentage-of-completion accounting, how much revenue is recognized in the first half-year?
options: $0 — no revenue until the contract is 100% complete
$2,000,000 — all revenue is recognized when the contract is signed
$1,000,000 — 50% of $2M, recognizing revenue proportionally to work completed
$500,000 — only the initial deposit is recognized
explanation: Percentage-of-completion recognizes revenue based on project completion: 50% complete × $2M = $1M in H1. This matches revenue to the period in which work was actually performed — the accrual principle applied to long-term contracts. Waiting until 100% completion would misstate financial performance by showing $0 revenue for 6 months of real economic activity.

question: Enron, WorldCom, and Wirecard were massive accounting frauds that deceived investors and destroyed shareholder value. These cases led to the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (2002), which requires CEOs and CFOs to personally certify financial statement accuracy. Which accounting principle was most egregiously violated in these cases?
options: The conservatism principle — companies should understate assets
Materiality — companies were reporting immaterial transactions incorrectly

Faithful representation — financial statements are supposed to accurately represent economic reality; these companies reported fabricated or manipulated numbers that misled investors about the true financial condition of the firm
The going concern principle — companies were overstating their survival probability
explanation: Faithful representation is accounting's core integrity requirement: financial statements must represent economic reality accurately, completely, and without bias. Enron created off-balance-sheet entities to hide debt; WorldCom capitalized $3.8B in expenses to show fake profits; Wirecard fabricated $2B in cash. All violated the fundamental principle that accounting reports must faithfully represent what actually happened.

question: A startup's CFO says: 'Our income statement shows $800K profit this year.' The CEO responds: 'But why is our bank account down $200K?' Which accounting concept explains this apparent contradiction?
options: The company must have made accounting errors in the income statement

Accrual accounting recognizes profit when earned, not when cash arrives — the $800K profit includes revenue from customers who haven't paid yet (accounts receivable) and excludes cash used to pay down debt or purchase assets; profit and cash flow are different measurements of the same business
The bank must have made an error
The CEO is confusing accounts payable with cash balances
explanation: This is the profit vs. cash flow distinction — one of the most important and most misunderstood concepts in business finance. Accrual profit can be $800K while cash decreases if: receivables increased (sales made but not collected), inventory built up, capital was invested in equipment, or debt principal was repaid. The cash flow statement reconciles these differences.

question: A private equity firm is evaluating an acquisition. They receive the target company's GAAP financial statements showing $12M EBITDA. The PE firm's analysts then adjust for: (1) $1.5M in non-recurring legal fees, (2) $800K in above-market rent paid to a related party, and (3) $600K in owner perks run through the business. What 'adjusted EBITDA' results, and why do PE firms make these adjustments?
options: $12M — GAAP numbers are already accurate and need no adjustment
$14.9M — GAAP EBITDA plus the three adjustments ($12M + $1.5M + $0.8M + $0.6M)
$15.9M — GAAP EBITDA of $12M plus all adjustments totaling $2.9M
Adjusted EBITDA is always lower than GAAP EBITDA
explanation: Adjusted EBITDA = $12M + $1.5M (non-recurring legal) + $0.8M (related-party rent overpayment) + $0.6M (owner perks) = $14.9M. PE firms add back non-recurring items and non-arm's-length transactions to estimate normalized earnings — what the business would earn under standard operating conditions as a standalone entity. This normalized figure is the basis for valuation multiples.

question: A publicly traded company's stock drops 15% when it announces earnings. The earnings were actually positive ($2 per share), but analysts had expected $2.40 per share. Which accounting and market concept explains this reaction?
options: The company must have reported incorrect numbers to cause a decline

Relative performance vs. expectations matters as much as absolute numbers — markets price stocks based on expected future earnings; when actual results fall short of expectations (even if positive), it signals the future earnings trajectory is lower than projected, causing a repricing
Stock prices can only fall when earnings are negative
Analyst expectations have no relationship to stock prices
explanation: Markets are forward-looking: stock prices reflect expected future earnings streams, not current earnings alone. Missing expectations ($2 vs $2.40 expected) signals that the growth trajectory is below what was priced in — causing a repricing of future cash flows. This is why companies manage earnings guidance carefully: the financial reporting function directly affects the company's stock price and cost of capital.

question: A company's CFO is under pressure from the CEO to 'smooth' earnings by accelerating recognition of future revenue this quarter to hit targets, then deferring this quarter's expenses to next quarter. Why is this problematic, even if it doesn't technically violate GAAP?
options: Earnings smoothing is always beneficial to investors

Earnings management that uses accounting flexibility to create misleading impressions of financial performance undermines the faithful representation principle even when technically permitted — investors relying on the numbers make worse decisions; over time, it usually requires larger manipulations to sustain
GAAP permits this practice without any consequences
Only large companies face scrutiny for earnings management
explanation: GAAP has flexibility (legitimate accounting estimates, timing choices) that can be used responsibly or manipulated. Earnings management that misleads investors about the true trend of business performance — even without technical violations — erodes the integrity of financial reporting. The SEC focuses on 'materiality' and intent; aggressive earnings management often escalates until it becomes fraudulent.

question: A nonprofit hospital and a for-profit hospital next door both treat identical patient populations with identical costs and revenues. Which accounting and reporting differences will appear in their financial statements?
options: Their financial statements will be identical since operations are identical

The nonprofit reports under GAAP with fund accounting, shows no income tax expense, and any surplus is reported as 'change in net assets' rather than 'net income'; the for-profit reports income tax expense (potentially 21%+ on profits), shows earnings per share, and reports distributable net income — the structural differences in tax treatment and equity accounting reflect their different ownership and mission structures
For-profit hospitals earn more revenue so their statements will differ
Nonprofits don't need to prepare financial statements
explanation: Entity type shapes financial reporting. The nonprofit pays no income tax, uses 'net assets' (not equity), and uses restricted/unrestricted fund categories. The for-profit shows income tax, retained earnings, and earnings per share. Same operations, different reporting frameworks — reflecting the different legal obligations and stakeholder relationships each faces. This illustrates how accounting serves different purposes for different organizational forms.

question: A startup CEO tells a VC: 'We reported $3M in revenue last quarter.' The VC asks: 'Is that GAAP revenue or booked revenue?' Why is this distinction important?
options: GAAP revenue and booked revenue are always identical

Booked revenue (contracts signed, orders placed) may not equal GAAP revenue (revenue recognized when earned/delivered) — a $3M contract signed in December for services delivered over 12 months is 'booked' but only $250K is GAAP revenue in December; misrepresenting booked contracts as recognized revenue overstates current period performance
VCs always prefer booked revenue over GAAP revenue as a metric
The distinction only matters for publicly traded companies
explanation: Revenue recognition timing is one of the most important dimensions of startup accounting. 'Booked' (Annual Contract Value, or ACV) and 'recognized' (what GAAP records in the period) can differ substantially for SaaS and services businesses. Investors in early-stage companies must specify which metric they're discussing to avoid confusion. Presenting bookings as recognized revenue is misleading — a common startup financial communication failure.

question: An investor is comparing two companies: Company A earns $10M net income but has deteriorating receivables (customers are paying slowly). Company B earns $8M net income but converts almost all profit to cash quickly. Which company does an astute investor prefer and why?
options: Company A — $10M net income is always better than $8M

Company B — cash conversion quality matters as much as reported profit; Company A's $10M profit may be partly uncollectable (bad debt risk) or will be collected late; Company B's $8M that converts immediately to cash compounds more effectively and has no collection risk; accounting quality analysis looks at cash conversion, not just the income statement number
Both companies are equivalent — net income is the definitive measure of financial health
Company A because size of profit always correlates with financial quality
explanation: Earnings quality analysis: not all $1 of reported profit is equal. Profits that convert quickly to cash are more valuable than profits that sit as receivables. Company A's $10M may include $3M in aging receivables that default — leaving real cash profit of $7M. Company B's $8M is fully collected. The cash flow statement and working capital analysis reveal this quality difference invisible in net income alone.
